mission.title: Install and Configure Nats on Kubernetes
mission.description: NATS is a high-performance messaging system designed for cloud and edge native applications. Installing NATS allows you to build distributed systems that are simple, secure, and efficient, making it an essential tool for modern application architectures.

steps.title: Add the NATS Helm repository
steps.description:
First, add the NATS Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration to access the latest charts.
```bash
helm repo add nats https://nats-io.github.io/k8s/helm/charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install NATS using Helm
steps.description:
Now, install NATS with the specified version in a new namespace called 'nats'.
```bash
helm install nats nats/nats --version 2.12.4 --namespace nats --create-namespace
```

steps.title: Verify the NATS installation
steps.description:
Check that the NATS pods are running successfully in the 'nats' namespace.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace nats
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
To ensure optimal performance, configure resource limits for the NATS deployment. Edit the deployment as follows:
```bash
kubectl edit deployment nats --namespace nats
```
Then, under the containers section, add:
```yaml
resources:
  limits:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "512Mi"
```

steps.title: Port-forward to access NATS
steps.description:
To access the NATS server locally, set up port forwarding:
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/nats --namespace nats 4222:4222
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation will show the NATS pods running in the 'nats' namespace and allow you to access the NATS server via port 4222 on your local machine. You can further configure resource limits to optimize your deployment.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add nats https://nats-io.github.io/k8s/helm/charts
helm repo update

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and both Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
